food_name: Nasi Ambeng (Javanese-Malay Communal Festive Rice Platter)
region_of_origin: Javanese-Malay community, Singapore (Kampong Glam and Geylang); Javanese origin, Indonesia
food_subgroup: Communal festive rice platter with multiple Malay side dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Nasi Ambeng. Closest parent is a mixed rice dish with multiple side dishes (communal festive rice platter). No exact FoodOn URI can be assigned without fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Communal festive rice platter with multiple Malay side dishes (Javanese-Malay nasi ambeng style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A00PB is the closest FoodEx2 parent for composite rice-based dishes. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Nasi Ambeng specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Nasi Ambeng or an equivalent Javanese-Malay communal rice platter.

allergens: gluten (possible from soy sauce in ayam masak kicap)

serving.natural_language_portion: One individual serving of Nasi Ambeng is approximately 420g, comprising a portion of steamed white rice (~180–200g) surrounded by a selection of side dishes including fried chicken, sambal goreng, serunding, bergedil, and a hard-boiled egg, traditionally shared communally from a large tray by 4–6 people.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Ambeng — IMR Malaysia MyFCD; adapted from HPB Singapore component data
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia (MyFCD); Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from IMR Malaysia MyFCD composite data for Nasi Ambeng, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore component-level data for individual side dishes (ayam goreng, rendang, sambal goreng, serunding, bergedil). Values represent a weighted average of the full platter composition per 100g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Rice and Mixed Rice Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nasi Ambeng as a composite dish. GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for jasmine/white rice (GI ~73) moderated by the substantial protein content from fried chicken and beef rendang, dietary fat from coconut-based serunding and rendang, and dietary fibre from tempeh, long beans, and potato bergedil. The mixed-macronutrient composition of the platter is well-established to reduce the glycaemic response of white rice. GL per 420g serving calculated as (62 × 90g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 55.7, reflecting the high carbohydrate load of a full individual portion.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from multiple sources: belacan-based sambal goreng, serunding, ayam goreng marinade, and bergedil seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 420g individual portion of Nasi Ambeng delivers approximately 1,596mg sodium — exceeding 69% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300mg — and patients with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced serunding, avoid additional table sambal, and treat this as an occasional festive food rather than a regular meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load (GL ~55.7) per individual portion driven by a large white rice component (~180–200g jasmine rice, GI ~73)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should reduce the rice portion to approximately 100g (halving carbohydrate intake to ~45g and GL to ~28), increase the proportion of protein (chicken, egg) and fibre-rich components (tempeh, long beans), and note that the isoflavones in tempeh may modestly improve insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (4.2g/100g; ~17.6g per 420g serving) from coconut-based serunding and fried chicken, combined with high sodium and dietary cholesterol (58mg/100g; ~244mg per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Nasi Ambeng to occasional consumption, request reduced serunding (the primary source of saturated fat and sodium), choose ayam goreng over beef rendang to reduce saturated fat intake, and be aware that the trans fat content (0.1g/100g) from frying contributes additional cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory phytochemicals from tempeh (soy isoflavones), lemongrass, and galangal in sambal goreng partially offset the pro-inflammatory potential of saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Nasi Ambeng is generally suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis in moderate portions; the tempeh component provides soy isoflavones with documented anti-inflammatory properties, and the aromatic herbs (lemongrass, galangal) contribute additional anti-inflammatory phytochemicals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Ambeng is a traditional Javanese-Malay communal festive rice dish deeply associated with the Kampong Glam neighbourhood of Singapore and the broader Javanese-Malay diaspora community. The dish is presented on a large wooden tray (talam) or banana leaf, with a generous mound of fragrant steamed jasmine rice at the centre, encircled by an array of richly flavoured side dishes: golden ayam goreng berempah (deep-fried spiced chicken), beef rendang (slow-braised coconut-spiced beef), sambal goreng (stir-fried tempeh, tofu, and long beans in a belacan-based chilli paste), serunding kelapa (dry-toasted spiced coconut floss), bergedil (pan-fried potato and minced meat patties), and a whole hard-boiled egg. Traditionally, four to six people sit around the tray and eat together using their right hands, sharing from the communal platter — a practice that reinforces Javanese-Malay communal food culture and social bonding. The dish is served at kenduri (religious feasts), Malay weddings, Hari Raya celebrations, and Ramadan gatherings. Nutritionally, Nasi Ambeng is a calorie-dense, protein-moderate, high-sodium dish: per 100g it provides approximately 195 kcal, 8.5g protein, 9.8g fat (4.2g saturated), and 21.5g carbohydrate. A full 420g individual portion delivers approximately 819 kcal, 35.7g protein, 41.2g fat, 90.3g carbohydrate, and 1,596mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at 62 (medium), moderated by the protein, fat, and fibre from the side dishes, but the glycaemic load per serving is very high (~55.7), making portion control essential for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is halal and dairy-free but contains soy, egg, and potential shellfish allergens from belacan.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from IMR Malaysia MyFCD composite data adapted with HPB Singapore component-level data. No single published laboratory analysis of Nasi Ambeng as a complete dish exists in a primary approved source. The composition of Nasi Ambeng varies significantly between vendors and occasions (ratio of rice to side dishes, specific side dish selection). GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI moderated by mixed macronutrient composition. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: All core macros (energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a mixed rice-and-side-dish platter. No zero-macro justification is required. Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat are reported as -1.0 as these were not reported in the source data. Water content is reported as -1.0 as not reported in source. The two portions in the input (420g and 500g) have been consolidated to a single default serving of 420g (verified source) as the primary individual portion; the 500g variant is noted as an alternative serving size. The input had two entries with is_default: true — corrected to a single default. FoodEx2 code A00PB is used as the closest defensible parent for composite rice dishes; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Nasi Ambeng. FoodOn URI is null as no specific FoodOn concept exists. SNOMED CT is null as no applicable clinical food concept exists. Hindi local name (नासी अम्बेंग) is a transliteration of the Malay name into Devanagari script, as no established Hindi-specific name exists for this Singaporean dish. The glycaemic load of 55.7 per 420g serving is very high and clinically significant for diabetes and metabolic management.
